params.text:
Balancing safety with rights and choices

People have the right to make their own decisions about their lives — including decisions that carry some risk. This is sometimes called the dignity of risk: the idea that being allowed to take everyday risks is part of living a full and dignified life. A person who is never allowed to try, to fail, or to make an unwise choice is being denied their independence.

This can sit in tension with your duty of care. Your instinct may be to protect someone from every possible harm, but over-protecting a person can strip away their confidence, dignity and self-worth. The skill lies in finding a balance.

Positive risk-taking is how we manage that balance. It means supporting a person to do the things they want to do, while sensibly assessing and reducing the risks — rather than simply saying "no".

How to support informed choices

When someone wants to do something that carries risk, you can support them safely by:

  • making sure they have clear, honest information about the possible benefits and dangers
  • listening to what matters to them and respecting their wishes
  • working with them, their family and the wider team to agree a plan and a risk assessment
  • recording the decision, the risks discussed and the agreed support

Remember that a person who has mental capacity has the right to make a decision that others might consider unwise. Your role is to support the choice safely, not to overrule it.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Key ideas about rights, choices and risk. Tap each card to reveal the explanation.

dialogs.answer: The principle that people have the right to take everyday risks and make their own choices, because doing so is part of living a full, dignified and independent life.
Supporting a person to do what they want to do while sensibly assessing and reducing the risks, rather than simply refusing or over-protecting them.

dialogs.text: Can someone make a decision others think is unwise?
dialogs.answer: Yes. A person with mental capacity has the right to make choices that others might disagree with. Your job is to support the choice safely, not to overrule it.
Over-protecting a person can take away their confidence, dignity and independence. Removing all risk can cause harm of a different kind.
Give clear information, listen to the person, complete a risk assessment with the team, agree a plan, and record the decision and the support offered.
